It isn't something you can do just one time. It's important to give your customers a way to provide their feedback, whether or not they have complained to you. Soliciting feedback can go a long way towards developing a customer service relationship.
sources: SOURCE: SE LAP 130—Go Beyond the Sale (Customer Service in Selling)

questions.question: Greg is giving a sales presentation when one of his customers brings up a controversial topic. Greg doesn't agree with the customer's view. Since he's committed to projecting a service attitude, Greg should

options.D: change the subject or remain silent.

questions.explanation: Change the subject or remain silent. Good salespeople are careful to avoid discussing controversial topics. This means that if a customer brings up a touchy subject, a salesperson should remain silent or change the subject. Greg should definitely not argue with his customer, even if he doesn't agree with the customer's views. He also shouldn't look unhappy by frowning or shaking his head. Although Greg might be tempted to laugh off the situation to diffuse tension, he shouldn't risk offending the customer.
sources: SOURCE: SE LAP 115—Keep Them Loyal (Building Clientele)

questions.question: Which of the following is an external factor that affects a business's selling policies:
questions.explanation: Competitors' actions. Forces operating in the business environment over which the business has little or no control are known as external factors. The number of competitors, their services, and the prices they charge for similar goods or services can affect a company's selling policies. Corporate goals and objectives, product capacity, and research and development efforts are all internal factors.

questions.question: Perry wants to make sure he sells a particular cleaning product to his customers, so he doesn't mention that it's incredibly toxic and dangerous around small children and animals. Perry is guilty of
questions.explanation: Negligence. Negligence occurs when a salesperson doesn't do something necessary or required. When Perry fails to mention that his product is toxic, he isn't providing customers with enough information to make educated buying decisions. Bribery, slander, and libel are all illegal actions, but Perry isn't engaging in them in this example.
sources: SOURCE: SE LAP 129—Keep It Real—In Sales (Legal and Ethical Considerations in Selling)

questions.question: Acquiring product information is most likely to increase a salesperson's
questions.explanation: Confidence. Acquiring product information increases a salesperson's confidence. S/He knows how to answer customers' questions, show customers how the product meets their needs, etc. Acquiring product information won't necessarily increase the number of customers a salesperson has, his/her level of education, or his/her commission.
sources: SOURCE: SE LAP 131—Get Informed (Acquiring Product Information for Use in Selling)

questions.question: Which of the following is considered a product benefit:

options.A: A computer has a large monitor.
options.B: A company offers a variety of models.
options.C: A software program saves you time.
options.D: A pair of shoes is green.

questions.explanation: A software program saves you time. A benefit is the personal satisfaction or advantage that a customer wants from a product. It is how a feature helps a particular buyer. A software program's benefit is that it saves you time. A company that offers a variety of models, a computer that has a large monitor, and the color of a pair of shoes are all features. Features are physical characteristics or qualities of products. 2015 HS ICDC MARKETING CLUSTER EXAM—KEY 32
sources: SOURCE: SE LAP 113—Find Features, Boost Benefits (Feature-Benefit Selling)

questions.question: A marketer for a security system company understands that consumers buy the company's product because they are motivated by the desire for safety. However, a marketer for a high-end clothing line focuses on customers who are motivated by the desire for esteem. These marketers are using __________ to understand consumer behavior.
questions.explanation: Maslow's hierarchy of needs. Maslow's hierarchy of needs outlines five basic areas of human needs. Marketers can use this information to understand consumer behavior when buying. For example, consumers who want a security system are motivated by the desire for safety, while consumers who want high end clothing are likely motivated by the need for esteem. Physiological needs are the first level of Maslow's hierarchy of needs. Maslow's hierarchy of needs is not the same as market research or perceived probability. Retrieved November 20, 2014, from http://smallbusiness.chron.com/description-marketers- can-use-maslows-hierarchy-needs-39333.html
sources: SOURCE: Thompson, M. (2014). Description of how marketers can use Maslow's hierarchy of needs.
